coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots induced defect-rich NiFe Prussian blue analogue as an efficient electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution reaction
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Yin-Chen
Aulia, Sofiannisa
Yeh, Min-Hsin
Hsiao, Li-Yin
Tarigan, Angelina Melanita
Ho, Kuo-Chuan
coredata.dc:description: High energy resource demand has led to the rapid development of hydrogen as a clean fuel through electrolytic water splitting. The exploration of high-performance and cost-effective electrocatalysts for water splitting is a challenging task to obtain renewable and clean energy. However, the sluggish kinetics of oxygen evolution reaction (OER) greatly hindered its application. Herein, a novel oxygen plasma-treated graphene quantum dots embedded Ni-Fe Prussian blue analogue (O-GQD-NiFe PBA) is proposed as a highly active electrocatalysts for OER. Furthermore, the defect induced by GQD can provide an abundant lattice mismatch in the matrix of NiFe PBA, which further facilitates faster electron transport and kinetic performance. After optimization, the as-assembled O-GQD-NiFe PBA exhibits excellent electrocatalytic performance towards OER with a low overpotential of 259 mV for reaching a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and impressive long-term stability for 100 h in an alkaline solution. This work broadens the scope of metal–organic frameworks (MOF) and high-functioning carbon composite as an active material for energy conversion systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrocatalysis
Graphene quantum dots
Oxygen evolution reaction
Oxygen plasma treatment
Prussian blue analogues

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NiFe PBA and GQD-NiFe PBA
Preparation of O-NiFe PBA and O-GQD-NiFe PBA electrode by oxygen plasma treatment
Structural characterization of NiFe PBA and GQD-NiFe PBA
Electrochemical performance of O-GQD-NiFe PBA for oxygen evolution reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of NiFe PBA and NiFe PBA simulation. (b) SEM images of NiFe PBA.
(a) XRD patterns of GQD-NiFe PBA 1:25, GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20, GQD-NiFe PBA 1:15, GQD-NiFe PBA 1:10, and (b) Raman spectra of GQDs, NiFe PBA, and GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20.

SEM images of (a) GQD-NiFe PBA 1:25, (b) GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20, (c) GQD-NiFe PBA 1:15, and (d) GQD-NiFe PBA 1:10. (e) Schematic growth mechanism of NiFe PBA crystals with various mass ratios of GQDs to NiFe PBA.
TEM images of (a) GQDs and (b, c) GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20.
(a) TEM images of GQD-NiFe PBA (1:20) with information of d-spacing; (c) Raman spectra and (c) XRD patterns of pristine NiFe PBA and GQD-NiFe PBA (1:20).
(a) SEM image O-GQD-NiFe PBA (1:20); (b) XRD pattern of GQD-NiFe PBA (1:20) and O-GQD-NiFe PBA (1:20).
High-resolution XPS (a) survey spectra, (b) O 1 s spectra, (c) Ni 2p spectra, and (d) Fe 2p spectra of O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20.

(a) LSV curves, (b) Tafel slope plots, (c) EIS Nyquist plots, and (d) current density as a function of scan rate to calculate the double layer capacitances (CDL) of the as-synthesized O-NiFe PBA, O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:10, O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:15, O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20, and O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:25. (e) Chronoamperometry curve of O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 at the overpotential of 260 mV.
Schematic illustration of the formation process of O-GQD-NiFe PBA.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots induced defect-rich NiFe Prussian blue analogue as an efficient electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution reaction

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University, Taipei 10617, Taiwan (K.C. Ho); Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei 10607, Taiwan (M.H. Yeh).

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High energy resource demand has led to the rapid development of hydrogen as a clean fuel through electrolytic water splitting. The exploration of high-performance and cost-effective electrocatalysts for water splitting is a challenging task to obtain renewable and clean energy. However, the sluggish kinetics of oxygen evolution reaction (OER) greatly hindered its application. Herein, a novel oxygen plasma-treated graphene quantum dots embedded Ni-Fe Prussian blue analogue (O-GQD-NiFe PBA) is proposed as a highly active electrocatalysts for OER. Furthermore, the defect induced by GQD can provide an abundant lattice mismatch in the matrix of NiFe PBA, which further facilitates faster electron transport and kinetic performance. After optimization, the as-assembled O-GQD-NiFe PBA exhibits excellent electrocatalytic performance towards OER with a low overpotential of 259 mV for reaching a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and impressive long-term stability for 100 h in an alkaline solution. This work broadens the scope of metal–organic frameworks (MOF) and high-functioning carbon composite as an active material for energy conversion systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the rapid depletion of fossil fuels and the resulting serious environmental problems have drawn the widespread attention of researchers to develop renewable and clean energy resources [1–4]. Hydrogen (H2) fuel produced via the electrocatalytic water splitting method has been widely considered as a potential candidate due to its carbon–neutral, abundant, and sustainable energy sources [5–8]. The hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) are the most crucial aspects of electrochemical water splitting. Nevertheless, as a critical half-reaction, the OER is significantly hindered by its sluggish kinetics because of the complicated multi-step proton-coupled electron transfer process [9–12]. Hence, developing highly effective electrocatalysts is a crucial task to lower the energy barrier of OER. Precious-metal-based oxide materials (RuO2 and IrO2) are recognized as the most active electrocatalysts, but the exorbitant cost, poor stability, and rarity greatly hinder their extensive applications [13–16]. Apparently, developing high-efficiency, durable, and inexpensive catalysts for the OER is incredibly essential.

Over the last few decades, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), synthesized by organic linkers and metal-based nodes, have attracted great attention in many fields [17–19]. MOFs, benefiting from their high porosity, high surface area, well-defined structures, and compositional flexibility, have been intensively utilized as versatile precursors or sacrificial templates for obtaining energy materials [20–24]. Moreover, the electrochemical performance is significantly affected by the morphology and chemical composition of MOFs [25–27]. Prussian blue analogues (PBAs), a representative class of MOFs, have shown promising applications for preparing energy storage and conversion materials owing to their tunable metal composition characteristics and facile preparation with different kinds of morphologies [28–30]. For example, Yu et al. proposed an active OER electrocatalyst based on porous carbon-coated nickel phosphides (NiP) using Ni-based PBA as a template and reported a low overpotential of 300 mV [31]. Guo et al. utilized one-step phosphidation of a NiCoFe PBA to synthesize hollow porous heterometallic phosphide nanocubes as a highly robust and active electrocatalyst for water splitting and showed a low overpotential of 273 mV [32]. It is generally believed that designing hybridized catalysts can lead to synergistic effects, thus increasing their overall activity [33–36]. Various carbon materials have been combined with electrocatalysts to enhance the electrocatalytic ability for different applications, for example, oxygen reduction reaction and water splitting [37–39]. Liu et al. combined Co3O4 with multi-walled carbon nanotubes to obtain the composite material as a superior bi-functional electrocatalyst [40]. Xu et al. reported the penroseite (Ni, Co)Se2 nanocages anchoring on graphene aerogel synthesized from NiCo PBA precursor as a competitive candidate for overall water splitting electrolysis [41]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), one of the latest additions to nanocarbon materials, have emerged as an advanced material for extensive applications [42]. GQDs are flat zero-dimensional graphene material with lateral dimensions typically less than 10 nm [43]. Their distinctive properties, such as excellent dispersibility, high electrical conductivity, abundant active sites, edge sites, and tunable functionalities, provide new opportunities to synthesize efficient electrocatalysts [44]. For instance, Cirone et al. synthesized cobalt-graphene quantum dots composites toward the OER and attained a smaller overpotential of 490 mV compared to the CoNP catalyst, which required 590 mV, for reaching a current density of 10 mA cm−2 [45]. Hou et al. synthesized the graphene dots (GDs)-anchored Co-Ni phosphide nanosheet-assembled tubular array as OER electrocatalysts and attained a low overpotential of 287 mV for reaching a current density of ∼ 15 mA cm−2 [46]. Moreover, the density functional theory (DFT) calculations performed on the hybrid material of GQDs and Ni3S2 nanosheets have confirmed that introducing GQDs to the Ni3S2 nanosheets can accelerate the absorption of OER intermediates [47]. Hence, using GQDs as carbon material to form the nanocomposite is a promising method to improve the OER electrocatalytic performance.

In this study, GQDs are embedded in the Ni3[Fe(CN)6]2 (NiFe PBA) via the crystallization of NiFe PBA in the presence of GQDs (GQD-NiFe PBA), followed by the oxygen plasma treatment to obtain the needed electrocatalysts (O-GQD-NiFe PBA) for OER. A schematic illustration of the formation process of O-GQD-NiFe PBA is shown in Scheme 1
. Due to electrostatic interactions, the nickel ion from nickel nitrate hexahydrate adsorbs onto the carboxylic groups (–COOH) modified GQDs. To obtain GQD-NiFe PBA composites, subsequent addition of potassium hexacyanoferrate (III) is required for heterogeneous crystallization/self-assembly. The composite of O-GQD-NiFe PBA with oxygen plasma treatment exhibits excellent electrocatalytic performance towards OER. Comparing with O-NiFe PBA, the outstanding OER performance of O-GQD-NiFe PBA resulted from the faster electron transport, larger defect sites, and greater lattice mismatch via introducing the GQD.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was obtained from Conjutek Co., Ltd. Nickel nitrate hexahydrate (≥98%) was obtained from Alfa Aesar. Sodium citrate tribasic dihydrate (≥99%), potassium hydroxide (KOH), isopropanol (IPA), and a NafionTM 117 solution (∼5%) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich. Potassium hexacyanoferrate(III) (≥99%) was obtained from Wako.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NiFe PBA and GQD-NiFe PBA
ce:section.ce:para: For synthesizing NiFe PBA, nickel nitrate hexahydrate (0.174 g) and sodium citrate tribasic dihydrate (0.221 g) were dissolved in deionized water (DIW, 20 mL) to obtain solution 1. Then, potassium hexacyanoferrate (0.132 g) was dissolved in DIW (30 mL) to form solution 2. Subsequently, both solution 1 and solution 2 were mixed under vigorous stirring for 1 min and then aged for one day at room temperature. After being collected by centrifugation and washed with DIW, the precipitate was dried under a vacuum for one day. For synthesizing the GQD and PBA composite, the GQDs were dissolved into the solution with nickel nitrate hexahydrate (10 mL). After that, sodium citrate tribasic dihydrate and potassium hexacyanoferrate were dissolved in DIW individually to obtain solution 1 and 2, respectively. Then, solution 1 and 2 were poured into the solution of nickel nitrate hexahydrate with GQDs one by one. The mass ratios of GQDs to NiFe PBA differed from 1:10 to 1:25. After being aged for 1 day at room temperature, the resultant precipitate was washed and centrifuged with DIW.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of O-NiFe PBA and O-GQD-NiFe PBA electrode by oxygen plasma treatment
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the O-NiFe PBA and O-GQD-NiFe PBA electrode, the as-prepared catalyst suspension was prepared by dispersing 10 mg of catalyst in 1 mL of a solution containing 50 μL of NafionTM, 0.75 mL of isopropanol, and 0.2 mL DIW followed by ultrasonication for 0.5 h. The active electrode area of 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm was defined using Epoxy tapes (3 M Company, thickness of 60 μm). Then the Ni foam was dropped by the above suspension to prepare a loading amount of 4.0 mg cm−2 and dried at ambient temperature. After that, an oxygen plasma (FEMTO Beispiel Variante 3) is utilized to functionalize the as-prepared NiFe PBA and GQD-NiFe PBA. The electrical power of 90 W was applied, and the treating time was controlled at 2 h in the oxygen plasma environment.

XRD patterns were obtained using an X-ray powder diffractometer (Ultima IV, Rigaku). Raman spectra (Renishaw) were collected at room temperature with a laser excitation wavelength of 514 nm. The morphology of the samples was observed using a scanning electron microscope (Nova NanoSEM 230, FEI) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL Ltd.). The surface chemical condition of the as-synthesized materials was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific Theta Probe). The bond strength was obtained using Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Perkin Elmer Spectrum).

A CHI 440 electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Inc., Austin, TX, USA) was utilized to perform electrochemical measurements. A potentiostat/galvanostat (PGSTAT 30, Autolab Eco-Chemie, Utrecht, the Netherlands) was used to analyze electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). All the measurements were carried out at ambient temperature in O2-saturated 1.0 M aqueous KOH with a standard three-electrode configuration including a Pt foil counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) reference electrode. The potential vs. Ag/AgCl was converted to that vs. a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) using Equation
(1) :
(1)
E
R H E
=
E
A g / A g C l
+
0.059 p H
+
E
o
A g / A g C l
where Eo Ag/AgCl = 0.1976 V at 25 °C. The Ni foam (FuCell Co., Ltd., Taoyuan, Taiwan, thickness = 1.7 mm, porosity = 110 pores per inch (PPI)) was used as the working electrode. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was corrected with iR-drop and performed at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. EIS tests were conducted by applying an AC amplitude of 5 mV with a frequency loop from 0.01 Hz to 100,000 Hz. The electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) of the electrocatalysts was obtained from the Cdl by performing the (cyclic voltammetry) CV at scan rates of 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 mV s−1 in the potential window of 1.16 to 1.26 V vs. RHE (within a non-faradaic region). The long-term stability with the overpotential at which the current density is set at 10 mA cm−2 was evaluated by carrying out chronoamperometry measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of NiFe PBA and GQD-NiFe PBA
ce:section.ce:para:
The crystalline NiFe PBA has been synthesized. The crystallographic structure of NiFe PBA is confirmed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, as shown in Fig. 1
a. All of the diffraction peaks are remarkably matched with the simulated XRD pattern of Ni3[Fe(CN)6]2 crystals (JCPDS card no. 86-0501) reported in the literature [48], indicating the successful synthesis of the NiFe PBA sample. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was executed to reveal the structure and morphology of the obtained samples. Fig. 1 b shows the SEM image of bare NiFe PBA with a highly uniform cubic structure, with an average particle size of about 95 nm. The GQD-NiFe PBA with different mass ratios of GQDs to NiFe PBA has also been fabricated. The samples were denoted as GQD-NiFe PBA 1:10, GQD-NiFe PBA 1:15, GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20, and GQD-NiFe PBA 1:25 depending on the pre-determined mass ratios of GQDs to NiFe PBA.

Fig. 2
a shows the XRD patterns of GQD-NiFe PBA with various mass ratios. The GQD-NiFe PBA samples exhibit similar diffraction peaks to bare NiFe PBA. It should be mentioned here that the absence of characteristic peaks of GQDs mainly results from highly crystalline NiFe PBA containing GQDs. After adding the GQDs in the process of crystallization, the prominent peaks of NiFe PBA become weaker, especially for GQD-NiFe PBA 1:10. According to the previous literature,

this phenomenon may indicate more defects caused by lattice mismatching at the GQD/NiFe PBA interface, which are particularly active for OER [37] and the smaller particle size of GQD-NiFe PBA compared to bare NiFe PBA [49]. The presence of GQDs in GQD-NiFe PBA is determined by performing Raman spectroscopy, and the obtained spectra are presented in Fig. 2 b. The two prominent peaks of GQDs centered at 1595 and 1345 cm−1 in its Raman spectrum correspond to the G band and D band of GQDs, respectively [50]. The spectrum of NiFe PBA shows one peak at 2184 cm−1, which is consistent with the reported Raman spectrum of NiFe PBA [51]. Noticeably, the Raman spectrum of GQD-NiFe PBA possesses two more peaks, centered at 1595 and 1345 cm−1, compared to bare NiFe PBA, which are contributed from the GQDs presented in the composite. As a consequence, this result reveals the presence of GQDs within GQD-NiFe PBA. Figure S1 shows the bond strengths of GQD-NiFe PBA, NiFe PBA, and GQDs confirmed by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The FTIR spectrum of NiFe PBA possesses two main peaks centered at 2166 cm−1 and 2101 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibration peak of the cyano group in the Fe3+–CN-Ni2+ and Fe2+–CN-Ni3+ environments, respectively [52]. The OH bending and stretching vibration of the water molecules can be noted at the peaks around 1608 cm−1 and 3411 cm−1, respectively [53]. On the other hand, the peaks at 1725 cm−1 and 1615 cm−1 of GQDs correspond to the CO and CC of GQDs. Furthermore, the peak centered at 1075 cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching of the COC [37]. It is worth noting that besides the peaks from NiFe PBA itself, the spectrum of GQD-NiFe PBA shows a small peak at 1075 cm−1, contributed by the GQDs presented in the composite. This result confirms the presence of GQDs within GQD-NiFe PBA.

The SEM images of the as-synthesized GQD-NiFe PBA 1:25, GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20, GQD-NiFe PBA 1:15, and GQD-NiFe PBA 1:10 are shown in Fig. 3
a–d. As the amounts of GQDs increase, the particle sizes of NiFe PBA decrease because large quantities of GQDs may cause more lattice mismatch and influence the crystallization of NiFe PBA. These results are consistent with the XRD patterns, in which larger particle sizes result from a high level of crystallization. The GQD-NiFe PBA 1:30 is synthesized, as shown in Figure S2 , to clarify the proposed mechanism of crystallization.

Fig. 3 e schematically illustrates a possible mechanism for the crystallization of NiFe PBA on the functionalized GQDs in different mass ratios of GQDs to NiFe PBA. Here, a growth mechanism for the GQD-NiFe PBA is proposed. Firstly, the functionalized GQDs are uniformly dispersed in deionized water (DIW). The abundant negatively-charged carboxylic groups (–COOH) and hydroxyl groups (–OH) on the surface of GQDs provide binding sites to metal ions for crystallization. Specifically, the GQDs are dispersed in DIW in the presence of nickel nitrate hexahydrate, and owing to the electrostatic interactions, nickel ion is adsorbed onto the functional groups of GQDs. Subsequently, HOC(COONa)(CH2COONa)2·2H2O, acting as a chelating agent to make the crystallization of the coordination compound kinetically controllable, is put in to coordinate with nickel ion. Finally, adding K3[Fe(CN)6] to the solution enables heterogeneous crystallization [54]. The small NiFe PBA particle located on the surface of the GQDs serves as a crystal nucleation center for the growing of NiFe PBA. Consequently, the GQDs are surrounded by NiFe PBA particles during the aging process.

Furthermore, a transmission electron microscope (TEM) was employed to reveal the internal morphology of the composite. Fig. 4
a shows the TEM image of GQDs, revealing that the GQDs are comprised of spherical particles with an average size of 3–5 nm. It is worth mentioning that the nanoparticles are GQDs with a characteristic lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, which is consistent with the lattice spacing reported in the literature [37]. As a result, GQDs are indeed embedded into the NiFe PBA instead of mixing physically with NiFe PBA, thus obtaining a novel GQD-NiFe PBA composite. The TEM images of the obtained GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 are shown in Fig. 4 b, c, which further revealed that GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 contains many tiny interconnected nanoparticles and possesses abundant lattice mismatch. The calculated d-spacings of GQD-NiFe PBA (1:20) by the TEM image (Fig. 5
a) visualize how the GQD could induce the lattice mismatch by altering the d-spacing of NiFe PBA. The region with less GQD has a d-spacing of 0.25 nm, corresponding to the (400) plane of the PBA. Meanwhile, the PBA region near the GQD shows a higher d-spacing of 0.27 nm. Besides, from the Raman spectra in Fig. 5 b, it was found that the peak between 2150 and 2200 cm−1 (which belongs to the –CN– bond of PBA) has shifted into a higher frequency. This shift was also found in the previous study, which suggests that a stretch in the –CN– bond happens [55]. Moreover, according to the XRD patterns (Fig. 5 c), there is a shift to a lower degree in the (400) plane of GQD-NiFe PBA (1:20), as compared to that of the NiFe PBA. According to a recent study, the shift could infer a change in a higher lattice (a mismatch) [56]. Thus, the desirable lattice mismatch between GQDs and NiFe PBA was successfully introduced to further contribute to the electrocatalytic performance.

Through the treatment of oxygen plasma, the NiFe PBA, GQD-NiFe PBA 1:10, GQD-NiFe PBA 1:15, GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20, and GQD-NiFe PBA 1:25 are designated as O-NiFe PBA, O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:10, O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:15, O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20, and O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:25, respectively. The SEM image of O-GQD-NiFe PBA (1:20) in Fig. 6
a depicts the retained morphology of GQD-PBA after the oxygen plasma treatment. The SEM-EDS result ( Table S1 ) was obtained to confirm the presence of oxygen in the final product of O-GQD-NiFe PBA (1:20). Moreover, Fig. 6 b shows the XRD patterns of GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 and O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 to confirm the structures before and after oxygen plasma treatment. It can be concluded that the XRD pattern of O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 remains the same as that of GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20. As a result, the O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 still possesses highly porous structure. Owing to the highly reactive oxygen species generated by oxygen plasma treatment, the metal sites of GQD-NiFe PBA are activated via oxygen bonding to it [65].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was done to characterize the valance states of the elements and chemical compositions on O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 surface. The survey XPS spectrum of O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 shows signals of elemental C, O, N, Ni, and Fe (Fig. 7
a). The high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1 s in Fig. 7 b shows a major peak at about 531.2 eV, which can be assigned to the oxygen species adsorbed on the surface. After the oxygen plasma treatment, there is another O 1 s peak at a lower binding energy of 529.5 eV, resulting from the oxygen binding to metal cations [57]. The high-resolution XPS of Ni 2p and Fe 2p spectra are shown in Fig. 7 c, and d, respectively. The peaks are deconvoluted into two peaks in the Ni 2p spectra corresponding to Ni 2p3/2 at 856.2/857.0 eV and Ni 2p1/2 at 873.6/874.7 eV, and these signals are assigned to the two valence states of Ni3+ and Ni2+ [58]. The satellite peaks of Ni are revealed in the broad peaks at 863.2 and 880.5 eV [59]. Besides, the two resolved peaks in Fe 2p spectra are assigned to Fe 2p1/2 at 721.4/723.8 eV and Fe 2p3/2 at 708.7/710.3 eV, which are ascribed to Fe3+ and Fe2+ accompanied by a satellite peak at 713.6 eV [60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of O-GQD-NiFe PBA for oxygen evolution reaction
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrocatalytic activities of the as-synthesized samples for OER were first evaluated by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) utilizing a typical three-electrode electrochemical system in a 1.0 M KOH aqueous electrolyte at room temperature. Fig. 8
a and Figure S5 show the polarization curves of O-NiFe PBA and O-GQD-NiFe PBA with different mass ratios of GQDs to NiFe PBA and bare Ni foam, respectively. The overpotential at specific current density is the most influential factor in judging the performance of OER electrocatalysts. To reach a current density of 10 mA cm−2, which is a normal standard to check the activity toward OER, the O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 exhibits a low overpotential of 259 mV. By comparison, the O-NiFe PBA, O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:10, O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:15, O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:25, and Ni foam need higher overpotentials of 322, 263, 260, 271, and 389 mV, respectively, for achieving the same current density of 10 mA cm−2. After adding GQDs, the overpotential decreases dramatically, suggesting the improved electrocatalytic activity contributed by the lattice mismatch at the GQD/NiFe PBA interface and the high electrical conductivity of GQDs. Moreover, the optimal mass ratio of GQDs to NiFe PBA is required to obtain the smallest overpotential for OER. Although the overpotentials of O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:10, O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:15, and O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 are quite similar, their performance has apparent disparity at high current density ( Figure S7 ). This result indicates that these three samples are all highly reactive, but excess amounts of GQDs, such as the mass ratio of 1:10, may sacrifice the performance contributed by NiFe PBA.

The OER reaction kinetics was evaluated using the Tafel slopes obtained from the LSV curves per the Tafel equation (η = b log j + a, where η is the overpotential, b is the Tafel slope, and j is the current density). A more rapid OER rate comes from faster electron transport, resulting in a smaller Tafel slope. As shown in Fig. 8 b and Figure S6 b, O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 exhibits a smaller Tafel slope of 52.5 mV dec−1 than O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:15 (55.6 mV dec-1), O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:10 (60.9 mV dec−1), O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:25 (86.2 mV dec-1), O-NiFe PBA (99.2 mV dec-1), and bare Ni foam (114.1 mV dec−1), which suggests the most favorable catalytic kinetics of O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20. In addition, the interfacial resistance of the electrocatalysts was analyzed by performing the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement. Fig. 8 c and Figure S6 c present the Nyquist plots of the electrocatalysts with the equivalent circuit and bare Ni foam, in which the charge transfer resistance (Rct) at the electrode/electrolyte interface is mainly attributed to the revealed semicircle. A smaller Rct value can improve the electrochemical performance of the electrode materials, as seen from the summary of the resulting Rs and Rct values listed in Table S2 . The O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 exhibits a much smaller semicircular diameter than O-NiFe PBA, O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:10, O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:15, O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:25, and bare Ni foam, indicating the fastest ion transport and electron transfer occurring at the O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20/electrolyte interface, thus resulting in the superior OER kinetics. Besides, the EIS results are consistent with the overpotential and Tafel slope values.

To better reveal the enhanced OER electrochemical activity of O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20, the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA), which is related to the exposed active sites, was estimated by measuring the electrochemical double-layer capacitance (CDL). CDL was measured by analyzing the cycling voltammograms (CV) at various scan rates between 0.15 V and 0.25 V vs. Ag/AgCl, where no redox peaks appeared. Figure S8 reveals the CV curves of the O-NiFe PBA, O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:10, O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:15, O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20, and O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:25. Fig. 8 d was obtained from the CV curves, displaying the corresponding fitting linear slopes. The fitting linear slope, which is proportional to the ECSA, is used to calculate the value of CDL for the specific electrode. Based on the results, the O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 possesses the largest CDL (11.97 mF cm−2) compared to O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:15 (11.1 mF cm−2), O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:10 (10.39 mF cm−2), O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:25 (9.56 mF cm−2), and bare O-NiFe PBA (5.02 mF cm−2). The O-GQD-NiFe PBA has larger CDL values despite various mass ratios compared with O-NiFe PBA, representing much more active surface area and abundant active sites after adding GQD. Particularly, the O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 has the highest value of CDL, indicating much more active metal sites exposed on the electrocatalyst surface, thus leading to the highest OER electrocatalytic activity among all samples. The turnover frequency (TOF) value of the O-GQD-PBA (1:20) catalyst was calculated based on the η = 259 mV with the formula: TOFOER = (j × S)/(4 × F × n) [61]. The current density (j) was taken at the 259 mV overpotential, S is the area of dropped catalyst on the nickel foam, 4 is the number of electrons to produce one mole of oxygen, F is the Faraday constant of 96485.3C mol−1, and n is the number of active sites in mole (the active metals in our study are Ni and Fe). The calculation would become TOF = (10 × 10-3 × 1)/(4 × 96485.3 × 2.06 × 10-5) = 0.001 s−1. For an energy conversion system, the robust stability of the electrocatalyst is also a key factor in addition to the electrocatalytic performance. The O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 in 1.0 M KOH alkaline condition with an applied potential at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 shows outstanding stability and maintains its catalytic activity for at least 100 h, as shown in Fig. 8 e, thus suggests an exceptional long-term durability of this material. Several characterizations were carried out after the chronoamperometric test to compare the OER performance as well as the physical characterization, such as SEM and XRD, to see the structural change of the O-GQD-NiFe PBA (1:20). All of the figures are shown in the revised supplementary file ( Figure S9 ). The SEM image of the O-GQD-NiFe PBA (1:20) after the long-term test ( Figure S9
b) indicates that the NiFe PBA cubical structure was reshaped into a larger irregular structure. Meanwhile, the XRD in Figure S9
c infers the collapsing characteristic peaks of NiFe PBA into a mixed state between Ni(OH)2, FeOOH, and Fe2O3, suggesting that the oxide and hydroxide state were formed after the chronoamperometric test. These states were formed due to the high activity of the catalyst, thus inducing the catalyst to form a higher oxidation state from each Ni and Fe [62–66]. Moreover, the LSV curves in Figure S9 d show a little change in the overpotential of the O-GQD-NiFe PBA (1:20). The slope of the LSV also suggests that the kinetic rate of the OER on the O-GQD-NiFe PBA might be slightly reduced after the long-term chronoamperometric test. Nevertheless, it is worth noticing that the active materials in O-GQD-NiFe PBA can still be utilized after the durability test. The performance of the O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 compares favorably to the other reported Ni-Fe-O-based electrocatalysts ( Table S3 ). Accordingly, these results of the O-GQD-NiFe PBA 1:20 indicate its emerging promise for diversified applications.

In summary, we have demonstrated the fabrication of oxygen plasma functionalized NiFe PBA with GQD modification as a potential noble-metal-free electrocatalyst for OER. Based on a recent study, introducing GQD into a transition-metal-based OER electrocatalyst could accelerate the absorption of OER intermediates [47]. In our study, the GQD was successfully incorporated into the NiFe PBA, which leads to the defect in the form of lattice mismatch confirmed by the physical characterization (TEM, Raman, and XRD). The defect induced by the GQD provides insight that could explain the interaction between the GQD and PBA, which has not been explored in previous studies. Moreover, the defect in the form of lattice mismatch was beneficial in increasing the activity of the catalyst towards OER by promoting faster electron transport, as supported from literature [37]. After optimization of the GQD ratio in the PBA matrix, the O-GQD-NiFe PBA (1:20) shows remarkable electrocatalytic performance with a low overpotential of 259 mV for achieving a current density of 10 mA cm−2, a Tafel slope as small as 52.5 mV dec-1, and the robust stability for over 100 h for OER in alkaline solution due to the faster electron transport and more defect sites induced by the GQD into the NiFe PBA. The mass ratio optimization of the GQD could clarify the influence of defects on the NiFe PBA performance. In addition, compared to the O-NiFe PBA, the improved electrocatalytic performance of the O-GQD-NiFe PBA further implies the significance of the GQD coupling with NiFe PBA. Overall, this work could shed some light on the possible interaction between MOF-based structures and high-functioning carbon composite, especially GQD, for an efficient OER electrocatalyst and energy conversion system.
Associated Content:
The manuscript was written with the contributions of all authors. All authors have approved the final version of the manuscript.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

Yin-Chen Lin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Sofiannisa Aulia: Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Min-Hsin Yeh: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Li-Yin Hsiao: Investigation, Validation. Angelina Melanita Tarigan: Investigation, Validation. Kuo-Chuan Ho: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) in Taiwan (MOST 111-2221-E-011-011, 110-2634-F-002-043, 110-2623-E-606-001, 111-3116-F-011-004, and 111-3116-F-002-005), National Taiwan University of Science and Technology & Institut Teknologi Sepuluh Nopember Joint Research Program (ITS-NTUST-112-05), and partially supported by the “Advanced Research Center of Green Materials Science and Technology” from the Featured Area Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education (112L9006). We also thank Ms. C.-Y. Chien of MOST (located at National Taiwan University) for assistance with the TEM experiments.

ce:section.ce:para: The supporting information contains figures regarding the characterization of the materials. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.05.187.
